OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Generalized OT Problems

The second family changes the optimization problem rather than only the ground distance. Barycenters average several measures, multi-marginal OT couples many measures at once, inverse OT learns the cost from observed transport, and weak OT allows randomized conditional responses.

These formulations remain close to Kantorovich linear programming, but the object being optimized is richer than a single two-marginal coupling.

Guiding Comparison

Barycenters optimize over the unknown measure being averaged. Multi-marginal OT optimizes over a whole joint law with several marginals. Inverse OT makes the ground cost the unknown. Weak OT keeps the marginal constraints but lets the cost depend on conditional laws rather than only on pointwise pairs.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

OT Barycenters¶

Barycenters ask how to average probability measures rather than points. This section explains the variational definition, the special closed forms in one dimension and for Gaussians, and the entropic algorithms used in practice.

Frechet Means¶

For discrete input histograms {bs}s=1S\{b_s\}_{s=1}^S{bs​}s=1S​, with bs∈Δnsb_s\in\simplex_{n_s}bs​∈Δns​​, and weights λ∈ΔS\lambda\in\simplex_Sλ∈ΔS​, a Wasserstein barycenter can be computed by minimizing

min⁡a∈Δn∑s=1Sλs LCs(a,bs),\min_{a\in\simplex_n} \sum_{s=1}^S \lambda_s\,\mathcal L_{C_s}(a,b_s),a∈Δn​min​s=1∑S​λs​LCs​​(a,bs​),
(1)

where the cost matrices Cs∈Rn×nsC_s\in\RR^{n\times n_s}Cs​∈Rn×ns​ are prescribed. This barycenter problem was introduced by Carlier and coauthors, following earlier matching ideas, and is the measure-valued analogue of a Frechet mean Agueh & Carlier, 2011Carlier & Ekeland, 2010.

Given input measures (βs)s(\beta_s)_s(βs​)s​ on a space X\XX, the barycenter problem is

min⁡α∈M+1(X)∑s=1Sλs Lc(α,βs).\min_{\alpha\in\mathcal M_+^1(\X)} \sum_{s=1}^S \lambda_s\,\mathcal L_c(\alpha,\beta_s).α∈M+1​(X)min​s=1∑S​λs​Lc​(α,βs​).
(2)

For X=Rd\X=\RR^dX=Rd and c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, if one input measure has a density, then the barycenter is unique Agueh & Carlier, 2011. Discrete existence, consistency, and fixed-point constructions are studied in Anderes et al., 2016Esteban et al., 2016Le Gouic & Loubes, 2016.

Example: Two measures recover a Wasserstein geodesic

For S=2S=2S=2, c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2 and weights (1−t,t)(1-t,t)(1−t,t), the barycenter is the point at time ttt on the Wasserstein geodesic between β0\beta_0β0​ and β1\beta_1β1​. If TTT is the Brenier map from β0\beta_0β0​ to β1\beta_1β1​, this barycenter is ((1−t)Id+tT)♯β0((1-t)\Id+tT)_\sharp\beta_0((1−t)Id+tT)♯​β0​, the McCann interpolation detailed in Section Geodesic Convexity and Convergence. If no Monge map is available, the same construction uses an optimal coupling π\piπ and the interpolation map (x,y)↦(1−t)x+ty(x,y)\mapsto(1-t)x+ty(x,y)↦(1−t)x+ty, giving ((1−t)x+ty)♯π((1-t)x+ty)_\sharp\pi((1−t)x+ty)♯​π.

Example: Dirac inputs recover Fr'echet means

Problem (2) generalizes the computation of barycenters of points (xs)s=1S∈XS(x_s)_{s=1}^S \in \X^S(xs​)s=1S​∈XS to arbitrary measures. Indeed, if βs=δxs\be_s=\de_{x_s}βs​=δxs​​ is a single Dirac mass, then a solution to (2) is δx⋆\de_{x^\star}δx⋆​ where x⋆x^\starx⋆ is a Frechet mean of the points (xs)s(x_s)_s(xs​)s​.

show_book_figure("barycenters-four-shapes")

Wasserstein barycenter grids for four corner measures. The left panel uses the one-dimensional formula Qu,v=∑i,jλij(u,v)QijQ_{u,v}=\sum_{i,j}\lambda_{ij}(u,v)Q_{ij}Qu,v​=∑i,j​λij​(u,v)Qij​ for one Gaussian law and three asymmetric two-Gaussian mixtures, and displays densities reconstructed from the averaged quantiles. The right panel computes entropic Wasserstein barycenters on a common pixel grid for the cat, two-disk, cross and clover silhouettes, using the normalized squared ground cost, ϵ=4⋅10−4\epsilon=4\cdot10^{-4}ϵ=4⋅10−4 and a Sinkhorn tolerance of 5⋅10−85\cdot10^{-8}5⋅10−8. The barycenters are rendered as density images with values clamped at their 95%95\%95% quantile rather than by threshold contours. Colors interpolate between the four corners and encode the same bilinear weights in both panels.

The interactive demo below keeps the exact one-dimensional formula visible: the two coordinates set bilinear weights on the four corner laws, the middle panel averages their quantile functions, and the right panel reconstructs the resulting barycenter density.

Interactive panel. Use the barycentric coordinate controls to move through the four input laws and compare quantile and entropic barycenter constructions.

Remark: Mean of a quadratic barycenter

For c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, the mean of the barycenter α⋆\al^\starα⋆ is necessarily the barycenter of the means,

∫Xx dα⋆(x)=∑sλs∫Xx dβs(x).\int_\Xx x \d\al^\star(x) = \sum_s \la_s \int_\Xx x \d\be_s(x).∫X​xdα⋆(x)=s∑​λs​∫X​xdβs​(x).
(3)

Indeed, the squared Wasserstein distance decomposes into a squared distance between means plus a centered Wasserstein term. Minimizing the resulting quadratic function of the barycenter mean gives the displayed identity. If the input measures have compact support, the usual multi-marginal barycentric construction also gives a barycenter supported in the convex hull of the input supports.

Proposition: Convexity of the OT Cost

The map (α,β)↦Lc(α,β)(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto\mathcal L_c(\alpha,\beta)(α,β)↦Lc​(α,β) is convex.

Proof

Let (α0,β0)(\alpha_0,\beta_0)(α0​,β0​) and (α1,β1)(\alpha_1,\beta_1)(α1​,β1​) be two pairs of probability measures and let t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1]. For η>0\eta>0η>0, choose couplings πi∈Π(αi,βi)\pi_i\in\Couplings(\alpha_i,\beta_i)πi​∈Π(αi​,βi​) such that

∫c dπi≤Lc(αi,βi)+η(i=0,1).\int c\d\pi_i \leq \mathcal L_c(\alpha_i,\beta_i)+\eta \qquad (i=0,1).∫cdπi​≤Lc​(αi​,βi​)+η(i=0,1).
(4)

Then πt=(1−t)π0+tπ1\pi_t=(1-t)\pi_0+t\pi_1πt​=(1−t)π0​+tπ1​ is a coupling between (1−t)α0+tα1(1-t)\alpha_0+t\alpha_1(1−t)α0​+tα1​ and (1−t)β0+tβ1(1-t)\beta_0+t\beta_1(1−t)β0​+tβ1​. Hence

Lc((1−t)α0+tα1,(1−t)β0+tβ1)≤(1−t)Lc(α0,β0)+tLc(α1,β1)+η.\mathcal L_c((1-t)\alpha_0+t\alpha_1,(1-t)\beta_0+t\beta_1) \leq (1-t)\mathcal L_c(\alpha_0,\beta_0) + t\mathcal L_c(\alpha_1,\beta_1) + \eta .Lc​((1−t)α0​+tα1​,(1−t)β0​+tβ1​)≤(1−t)Lc​(α0​,β0​)+tLc​(α1​,β1​)+η.
(5)

Letting η→0\eta\to0η→0 gives the claim.

Even when all input measures are discrete, the support of a barycenter is not known a priori. The multi-marginal formulation below shows that a discrete barycenter can be supported on all weighted averages of one support point from each input. This gives at most ∏sns\prod_s n_s∏s​ns​ candidate points if the sss-th input has nsn_sns​ atoms, which is prohibitive when the number of inputs is large. A common numerical compromise is therefore to prescribe a smaller support for the barycenter and solve a fixed-support problem.

One-Dimensional Case¶

On the line, barycenters become linear after the quantile change of variables. This gives the rare case where the barycenter is explicit rather than the solution of a high-dimensional optimization problem.

Proposition: Quantile Barycenters on the Line

For X=R\X=\RRX=R and c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2c(x,y)=|x-y|^2c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2, the quantile function of a Wasserstein barycenter is the weighted average of the input quantile functions:

Fα⋆−1(r)=∑s=1SλsFβs−1(r),r∈[0,1].F_{\alpha^\star}^{-1}(r) = \sum_{s=1}^S \lambda_s F_{\beta_s}^{-1}(r), \qquad r\in[0,1].Fα⋆−1​(r)=s=1∑S​λs​Fβs​−1​(r),r∈[0,1].
(6)
Proof

The one-dimensional formula for W2\Wass_2W2​ gives

∑sλsW22(α,βs)=∫01∑sλs∣Fα−1(r)−Fβs−1(r)∣2 dr.\sum_s\lambda_s\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta_s) = \int_0^1 \sum_s\lambda_s \abs{F_\alpha^{-1}(r)-F_{\beta_s}^{-1}(r)}^2 \d r.s∑​λs​W22​(α,βs​)=∫01​s∑​λs​∣∣​Fα−1​(r)−Fβs​−1​(r)∣∣​2dr.
(7)

The minimization decouples pointwise in rrr. For each fixed rrr, the minimizer of z↦∑sλs∣z−Fβs−1(r)∣2z\mapsto\sum_s\lambda_s|z-F_{\beta_s}^{-1}(r)|^2z↦∑s​λs​∣z−Fβs​−1​(r)∣2 is the weighted average ∑sλsFβs−1(r)\sum_s\lambda_sF_{\beta_s}^{-1}(r)∑s​λs​Fβs​−1​(r). This function is nondecreasing because it is a positive weighted sum of nondecreasing quantile functions, hence it is a valid quantile function.

Gaussian Case¶

Gaussian barycenters show that the same separation as in the Gaussian Wasserstein formula persists: means average linearly, while covariances average according to the Bures--Wasserstein geometry.

Example: Gaussian inputs remain Gaussian

The barycenter of Gaussian measures is Gaussian. In one dimension, it is obtained by averaging the means and the standard deviations, so the barycenter variance is the square of this averaged standard deviation. In higher dimensions, the covariance Σ\covΣ minimizes the Bures objective

Σ↦∑sλsB(Σ,Σs)2,\cov \mapsto \sum_s \la_s \Bb(\cov,\cov_s)^2,Σ↦s∑​λs​B(Σ,Σs​)2,
(8)

and equivalently solves the fixed-point equation

Σ=∑sλs(Σ1/2ΣsΣ1/2)1/2.\cov = \sum_s \la_s \pa{\cov^{1/2}\cov_s\cov^{1/2}}^{1/2}.Σ=s∑​λs​(Σ1/2Σs​Σ1/2)1/2.
(9)

This is the covariance analogue of the usual Euclidean barycenter equation: the mean part averages linearly, while the covariance part averages through the Bures--Wasserstein geometry Esteban et al., 2016Bhatia et al., 2019.

show_book_figure("barycenters-gaussian-covariances")

Bures--Wasserstein barycenters of centered Gaussian covariance matrices. Each panel shows a 5×55\times55×5 grid of barycenter ellipses for four corner covariances, without separate input panels: the corner ellipses are the four input covariances themselves. The right grid uses more anisotropic inputs, making the nonlinear rotation and scaling of covariance barycenters more visible.

The interactive Gaussian demo compares the Bures covariance barycenter with a plain Euclidean covariance average under the same weights. The difference is most visible for rotated, anisotropic covariances: the Euclidean average blends matrix entries, whereas the Bures barycenter follows the geometry induced by quadratic Gaussian transport.

Interactive panel. Use the corner-covariance and interpolation controls to see how Gaussian barycenter ellipses interpolate covariance geometry.

Sinkhorn for Barycenters¶

A key difference with the regularized two-marginal OT problem is that there is no canonical reference measure α⊗β\alpha\otimes\betaα⊗β, because the barycenter α\alphaα is unknown. To reduce complexity, one usually fixes a candidate support for the barycenter and solves the discrete problem (1); this introduces a discretization error but keeps the number of unknowns manageable.

One can then use entropic smoothing and approximate (1) by

min⁡a∈Δn∑s=1SλsLCsϵ(a,bs)\min_{a\in\simplex_n} \sum_{s=1}^S \lambda_s\mathcal L_{C_s}^{\epsilon}(a,b_s)a∈Δn​min​s=1∑S​λs​LCs​ϵ​(a,bs​)
(10)

for some ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0. This is a smooth convex minimization problem, which can be tackled using gradient descent Cuturi & Doucet, 2014. An alternative is to use a descent method, typically quasi-Newton, on the semi-dual Cuturi & Peyré, 2016; this is useful when adding extra regularization on the barycenter, for instance to impose smoothness.

A simple but effective approach rewrites (10) as a weighted KL projection problem Benamou et al., 2015:

min⁡(Ps)sϵ∑sλsKL⁡(Ps∣Ks)\min_{(P_s)_s} \epsilon\sum_s\lambda_s \operatorname{KL}(P_s\mid K_s)(Ps​)s​min​ϵs∑​λs​KL(Ps​∣Ks​)
(11)

subject to

Ps⊤1n=bsfor all s,P11n1=⋯=PS1nS.P_s^\top\mathbf 1_n=b_s \quad\text{for all }s, \qquad P_1\mathbf 1_{n_1} = \cdots = P_S\mathbf 1_{n_S}.Ps⊤​1n​=bs​for all s,P1​1n1​​=⋯=PS​1nS​​.
(12)

Here Ks:=e−Cs/ϵK_s\eqdef e^{-C_s/\epsilon}Ks​:=e−Cs​/ϵ. The barycenter aaa is implicitly encoded in the common row marginal

a=P11n1=⋯=PS1nS.a=P_1\mathbf 1_{n_1}=\cdots=P_S\mathbf 1_{n_S}.a=P1​1n1​​=⋯=PS​1nS​​.
(13)

The optimal couplings have scaling form

Ps=diag⁡(us)Ksdiag⁡(vs),P_s=\diag(u_s)K_s\diag(v_s),Ps​=diag(us​)Ks​diag(vs​),
(14)

and the generalized Sinkhorn iterations are

vs←bsKs⊤us,a←∏s(Ksvs)λs,us←aKsvs.v_s\leftarrow\frac{b_s}{K_s^\top u_s}, \qquad a\leftarrow\prod_s(K_s v_s)^{\lambda_s}, \qquad u_s\leftarrow\frac{a}{K_s v_s}.vs​←Ks⊤​us​bs​​,a←s∏​(Ks​vs​)λs​,us​←Ks​vs​a​.
(15)

The geometric mean enforces the fact that all couplings share the same barycenter marginal.

Proposition: Dual of Entropic Barycenters

The optimal scalings in (14) can be written as (us,vs)=(efs/ϵ,egs/ϵ)(u_s,v_s)=(e^{f_s/\epsilon},e^{g_s/\epsilon})(us​,vs​)=(efs​/ϵ,egs​/ϵ), where (fs,gs)s(f_s,g_s)_s(fs​,gs​)s​ solve

max⁡(fs,gs)s∑sλs(⟨gs,bs⟩−ϵ⟨Ksegs/ϵ,efs/ϵ⟩)subject to∑sλsfs=0.\max_{(f_s,g_s)_s} \sum_s\lambda_s \left( \dotp{g_s}{b_s} - \epsilon\dotp{K_s e^{g_s/\epsilon}}{e^{f_s/\epsilon}} \right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_s\lambda_s f_s=0.(fs​,gs​)s​max​s∑​λs​(⟨gs​,bs​⟩−ϵ⟨Ks​egs​/ϵ,efs​/ϵ⟩)subject tos∑​λs​fs​=0.
(16)
Proof

Introduce Lagrange multipliers in (11):

min⁡(Ps)s,amax⁡(fs,gs)s∑sλs(ϵKL⁡(Ps∣Ks)+⟨a−Ps1ns,fs⟩+⟨bs−Ps⊤1n,gs⟩).\min_{(P_s)_s,a} \max_{(f_s,g_s)_s} \sum_s\lambda_s \left( \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(P_s\mid K_s) + \dotp{a-P_s\mathbf 1_{n_s}}{f_s} + \dotp{b_s-P_s^\top\mathbf 1_n}{g_s} \right).(Ps​)s​,amin​(fs​,gs​)s​max​s∑​λs​(ϵKL(Ps​∣Ks​)+⟨a−Ps​1ns​​,fs​⟩+⟨bs​−Ps⊤​1n​,gs​⟩).
(17)

Strong duality allows one to exchange the minimum and maximum. Minimizing with respect to aaa gives the constraint ∑sλsfs=0\sum_s\lambda_s f_s=0∑s​λs​fs​=0, and minimizing with respect to PsP_sPs​ gives the Legendre transform of KL⁡(⋅∣Ks)\operatorname{KL}(\cdot\mid K_s)KL(⋅∣Ks​):

max⁡(fs,gs)s∑sλs[⟨gs,bs⟩−ϵKL⁡∗(fs⊕gsϵ|Ks)],∑sλsfs=0.\max_{(f_s,g_s)_s} \sum_s\lambda_s \left[ \dotp{g_s}{b_s} - \epsilon \operatorname{KL}^* \left(\frac{f_s\oplus g_s}{\epsilon}\middle|K_s\right) \right], \qquad \sum_s\lambda_s f_s=0.(fs​,gs​)s​max​s∑​λs​[⟨gs​,bs​⟩−ϵKL∗(ϵfs​⊕gs​​∣∣​Ks​)],s∑​λs​fs​=0.
(18)

The separable conjugate is

KL⁡∗(U∣K)=∑i,jKi,j(eUi,j−1),\operatorname{KL}^*(U\mid K) = \sum_{i,j}K_{i,j}(e^{U_{i,j}}-1),KL∗(U∣K)=i,j∑​Ki,j​(eUi,j​−1),
(19)

because for k>0k>0k>0,

sup⁡r≥0ur−(rlog⁡(r/k)−r+k)=k(eu−1).\sup_{r\geq0} ur-\big(r\log(r/k)-r+k\big) = k(e^u-1).r≥0sup​ur−(rlog(r/k)−r+k)=k(eu−1).
(20)

Dropping constants independent of the potentials gives the displayed dual. Coordinate maximization in gsg_sgs​ gives the vsv_svs​ update; block maximization in all (fs)s(f_s)_s(fs​)s​ gives the common marginal update and then the usu_sus​ update.

Classical applications include two-dimensional image interpolation, three-dimensional shape interpolation, and barycenters on surfaces where the ground cost is the square of the geodesic distance Solomon et al., 2015.

Algorithm: Gaussian barycenter fixed point

Input: Gaussian measures N(ms,Σs)\Gaussian(\mean_s,\cov_s)N(ms​,Σs​), weights λs\lambda_sλs​, tolerance tol\mathrm{tol}tol.

Output: Gaussian barycenter N(m,Σ)\Gaussian(\mean,\cov)N(m,Σ).

Set m=∑sλsms.\mean=\sum_s\lambda_s\mean_s .m=∑s​λs​ms​.

Initialize: Set Σ(0)=∑sλsΣs\cov^{(0)}=\sum_s\lambda_s\cov_sΣ(0)=∑s​λs​Σs​.

For k=0,1,…k=0,1,\ldotsk=0,1,… do:

S(k)=∑sλs((Σ(k))1/2Σs(Σ(k))1/2)1/2.S^{(k)} = \sum_s\lambda_s \left((\cov^{(k)})^{1/2}\cov_s(\cov^{(k)})^{1/2}\right)^{1/2}.S(k)=∑s​λs​((Σ(k))1/2Σs​(Σ(k))1/2)1/2.

Σ(k+1)=(Σ(k))−1/2(S(k))2(Σ(k))−1/2.\cov^{(k+1)} = (\cov^{(k)})^{-1/2} \left(S^{(k)}\right)^2 (\cov^{(k)})^{-1/2}.Σ(k+1)=(Σ(k))−1/2(S(k))2(Σ(k))−1/2.

If ∥Σ(k+1)−Σ(k)∥≤tol\norm{\cov^{(k+1)}-\cov^{(k)}}\leq\mathrm{tol}∥∥​Σ(k+1)−Σ(k)∥∥​≤tol then:

Set Σ=Σ(k+1)\cov=\cov^{(k+1)}Σ=Σ(k+1). Return N(m,Σ)\Gaussian(\mean,\cov)N(m,Σ).

Algorithm: Entropic barycenter Sinkhorn

Input: Costs Cs\C_sCs​, target weights bs\b_sbs​, barycenter weights λs\lambda_sλs​, regularization ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, tolerance tol\mathrm{tol}tol.

Output: Barycenter weights a\aa and couplings Ps\P_sPs​.

Initialize: Set Ks=e−Cs/ϵ\K_s=e^{-\C_s/\epsilon}Ks​=e−Cs​/ϵ, us(0)=1n\uD_s^{(0)}=\ones_nus(0)​=1n​ for all sss, r0=+∞r_0=+\inftyr0​=+∞, and k=0k=0k=0.

While rk>tolr_k>\mathrm{tol}rk​>tol do:

Set k←k+1k\leftarrow k+1k←k+1.

For each marginal sss do

vs(k)=bsKs⊤us(k−1).\vD_s^{(k)} = \frac{\b_s}{\transp{\K_s}\uD_s^{(k-1)}}.vs(k)​=Ks⊤​us(k−1)​bs​​.

Compute barycenter marginal: a(k)=∏s(Ksvs(k))λs.\a^{(k)} = \prod_s \bigl(\K_s\vD_s^{(k)}\bigr)^{\lambda_s}.a(k)=∏s​(Ks​vs(k)​)λs​.

For each marginal sss do

us(k)=a(k)Ksvs(k).\uD_s^{(k)} = \frac{\a^{(k)}}{\K_s\vD_s^{(k)}}.us(k)​=Ks​vs(k)​a(k)​.

Set Ps(k)=diag⁡(us(k))Ksdiag⁡(vs(k))\P_s^{(k)}=\diag(\uD_s^{(k)})\K_s\diag(\vD_s^{(k)})Ps(k)​=diag(us(k)​)Ks​diag(vs(k)​) for all sss.

Set rk=max⁡smax⁡{∥Ps(k)1−a(k)∥1,∥(Ps(k))⊤1−bs∥1}r_k=\max_s \max\{\norm{\P_s^{(k)}\ones-\a^{(k)}}_1,\norm{(\P_s^{(k)})^\top\ones-\b_s}_1\}rk​=maxs​max{∥∥​Ps(k)​1−a(k)∥∥​1​,∥∥​(Ps(k)​)⊤1−bs​∥∥​1​}.

Return a(k)\a^{(k)}a(k) and Ps(k)\P_s^{(k)}Ps(k)​.

Multimarginal OT¶

Multi-marginal OT couples more than two measures at once. It is the natural language for barycenters, matching with teams and several-body costs, but its tensor dimension is the main computational obstacle.

Definition and Basic Structure¶

The multi-marginal formulation replaces a coupling between two measures by a joint distribution with several prescribed marginals. Given measures (αs)s=1S(\alpha_s)_{s=1}^S(αs​)s=1S​ on spaces (Xs)s=1S(\X_s)_{s=1}^S(Xs​)s=1S​ and a cost c:X1×⋯×XS→Rc:\X_1\times\cdots\times\X_S\to\RRc:X1​×⋯×XS​→R, the problem reads

inf⁡π∈Π(α1,…,αS)∫X1×⋯×XSc(x1,…,xS) dπ(x1,…,xS),\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_S)} \int_{\X_1\times\cdots\times\X_S} c(x_1,\ldots,x_S)\d\pi(x_1,\ldots,x_S),π∈Π(α1​,…,αS​)inf​∫X1​×⋯×XS​​c(x1​,…,xS​)dπ(x1​,…,xS​),
(21)

where Π(α1,…,αS)\Couplings(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_S)Π(α1​,…,αS​) is the set of probability measures whose sss-th marginal is αs\alpha_sαs​. This is still a linear program in the discrete setting, but its ambient tensor has size ∏sns\prod_s n_s∏s​ns​.

Multi-Marginal Formulation of Barycenters¶

Wasserstein barycenters are the central example. For the squared Euclidean cost, one can introduce a latent barycenter point and eliminate it explicitly, leading to the multi-marginal cost

cbar(x1,…,xS)=min⁡x∈Rd∑s=1Sλs∥x−xs∥2.c_{\mathrm{bar}}(x_1,\ldots,x_S) = \min_{x\in\RR^d} \sum_{s=1}^S\lambda_s\norm{x-x_s}^2.cbar​(x1​,…,xS​)=x∈Rdmin​s=1∑S​λs​∥x−xs​∥2.
(22)
Proposition: Multi-Marginal Formula for Quadratic Barycenters

Let β1,…,βS∈P2(Rd)\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_S\in\mathcal P_2(\RR^d)β1​,…,βS​∈P2​(Rd) and λ∈ΔS\lambda\in\simplex_Sλ∈ΔS​. Define

B(x1,…,xS)=∑s=1Sλsxs,cbar(x1,…,xS)=min⁡x∑sλs∥x−xs∥2.B(x_1,\ldots,x_S)=\sum_{s=1}^S\lambda_s x_s, \qquad c_{\mathrm{bar}}(x_1,\ldots,x_S) = \min_x \sum_s\lambda_s\norm{x-x_s}^2.B(x1​,…,xS​)=s=1∑S​λs​xs​,cbar​(x1​,…,xS​)=xmin​s∑​λs​∥x−xs​∥2.
(23)

If π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆ solves the multi-marginal OT problem with marginals (βs)s(\beta_s)_s(βs​)s​ and cost cbarc_{\mathrm{bar}}cbar​, then α⋆=B♯π⋆\alpha^\star=B_\sharp\pi^\starα⋆=B♯​π⋆ is a Wasserstein barycenter. Conversely, every barycenter is obtained this way from an optimal multi-marginal plan.

Proof

For any candidate barycenter α\alphaα and couplings πs∈Π(α,βs)\pi_s\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta_s)πs​∈Π(α,βs​), glue the couplings along their common α\alphaα marginal to obtain a joint law of (X,Y1,…,YS)(X,Y_1,\ldots,Y_S)(X,Y1​,…,YS​). Conditioning on (Ys)s(Y_s)_s(Ys​)s​ and minimizing over XXX gives

∑sλsE∥X−Ys∥2≥Emin⁡x∑sλs∥x−Ys∥2=Ecbar(Y1,…,YS).\sum_s\lambda_s\mathbb E\norm{X-Y_s}^2 \geq \mathbb E \min_x \sum_s\lambda_s\norm{x-Y_s}^2 = \mathbb E c_{\mathrm{bar}}(Y_1,\ldots,Y_S).s∑​λs​E∥X−Ys​∥2≥Exmin​s∑​λs​∥x−Ys​∥2=Ecbar​(Y1​,…,YS​).
(24)

Taking the infimum over the couplings gives that the barycenter value is at least the multi-marginal value. Conversely, from an optimal multi-marginal plan π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆, set X=B(Y1,…,YS)X=B(Y_1,\ldots,Y_S)X=B(Y1​,…,YS​). The couplings between XXX and each YsY_sYs​ are feasible for the barycenter problem and attain exactly the multi-marginal cost.

If α⋆\alpha^\starα⋆ is any barycenter, choose optimal couplings between α⋆\alpha^\starα⋆ and each βs\beta_sβs​ and glue them along the common α⋆\alpha^\starα⋆ marginal. Since the barycenter and multi-marginal values are equal, the conditional minimization inequality above must be an equality. Thus X=B(Y1,…,YS)X=B(Y_1,\ldots,Y_S)X=B(Y1​,…,YS​) almost surely for the induced optimal multi-marginal plan.

Corollary: Gaussian and Discrete Barycenters

Quadratic Wasserstein barycenters of Gaussian measures are Gaussian. If the input measures are discrete, then there exists a barycenter supported on the set of weighted averages ∑sλsxs,is\sum_s\lambda_s x_{s,i_s}∑s​λs​xs,is​​ of one support point from each input. In particular, if the sss-th input has nsn_sns​ atoms, a barycenter exists with at most ∏sns\prod_s n_s∏s​ns​ atoms.

Proof

Let the input Gaussians have means msm_sms​ and covariances Σs\Sigma_sΣs​. For any candidate barycenter α\alphaα with mean mmm and covariance Σ\SigmaΣ, Gelbrich’s inequality gives

W22(α,βs)≥∥m−ms∥2+B(Σ,Σs)2,\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta_s) \geq \norm{m-m_s}^2+\mathcal B(\Sigma,\Sigma_s)^2,W22​(α,βs​)≥∥m−ms​∥2+B(Σ,Σs​)2,
(25)

with equality for the Gaussian law with mean mmm and covariance Σ\SigmaΣ. Therefore the barycenter objective is bounded below by a function depending only on (m,Σ)(m,\Sigma)(m,Σ), and this lower bound is attained by the Gaussian measure with the minimizing mean and covariance. For discrete inputs, any multi-marginal optimizer is supported on the finite product of the input supports, and BBB maps this product to at most ∏sns\prod_s n_s∏s​ns​ points.

Entropic Regularization of Multi-Marginal OT¶

As in the two-marginal case, adding an entropic penalty with respect to the product measure α1⊗⋯⊗αS\alpha_1\otimes\cdots\otimes\alpha_Sα1​⊗⋯⊗αS​ leads to scaling algorithms:

inf⁡π∈Π(α1,…,αS)∫c dπ+ϵKL⁡(π∣α1⊗⋯⊗αS).\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_S)} \int c\d\pi + \epsilon\operatorname{KL} (\pi\mid\alpha_1\otimes\cdots\otimes\alpha_S).π∈Π(α1​,…,αS​)inf​∫cdπ+ϵKL(π∣α1​⊗⋯⊗αS​).
(26)

The optimizer has the generalized Gibbs form

 dπ⋆(x1,…,xS)=exp⁡ ⁣(∑sfs(xs)−c(x1,…,xS)ϵ)∏s dαs(xs),\d\pi^\star(x_1,\ldots,x_S) = \exp\!\left( \frac{\sum_s f_s(x_s)-c(x_1,\ldots,x_S)}{\epsilon} \right) \prod_s\d\alpha_s(x_s),dπ⋆(x1​,…,xS​)=exp(ϵ∑s​fs​(xs​)−c(x1​,…,xS​)​)s∏​dαs​(xs​),
(27)

and generalized Sinkhorn iterations alternately update one potential fsf_sfs​ so that the sss-th marginal is correct. The bottleneck is the tensor size ∏sns\prod_s n_s∏s​ns​ in the discrete case. Practical barycenter solvers exploit separability of the cost, low-rank structure, convolutional kernels, or a fixed barycenter support.

Algorithm: Multi-marginal Sinkhorn

Input: Marginals as∈Δns\a_s\in\simplex_{n_s}as​∈Δns​​, tensor cost CCC, regularization ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, tolerance tol\mathrm{tol}tol.

Output: Multi-marginal entropic coupling tensor PPP.

Build Ki1,…,iS=exp⁡ ⁣(−Ci1,…,iSϵ)∏s=1S(as)is.K_{i_1,\ldots,i_S} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{C_{i_1,\ldots,i_S}}{\epsilon}\right) \prod_{s=1}^S(\a_s)_{i_s}.Ki1​,…,iS​​=exp(−ϵCi1​,…,iS​​​)∏s=1S​(as​)is​​.

Initialize: Set us=1nsu_s=\ones_{n_s}us​=1ns​​ for all sss and residual r=+∞r=+\inftyr=+∞.

While r>tolr>\mathrm{tol}r>tol do:

For s=1,…,Ss=1,\ldots,Ss=1,…,S do:

(us)i←(as)i∑i1,…,is−1,is+1,…,iSKi1,…,is−1,i,is+1,…,iS∏r≠s(ur)ir.(u_s)_i \leftarrow \frac{(\a_s)_i} { \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_{s-1},i_{s+1},\ldots,i_S} K_{i_1,\ldots,i_{s-1},i,i_{s+1},\ldots,i_S} \prod_{r\neq s}(u_r)_{i_r}}.(us​)i​←∑i1​,…,is−1​,is+1​,…,iS​​Ki1​,…,is−1​,i,is+1​,…,iS​​∏r=s​(ur​)ir​​(as​)i​​.

Set Pi1,…,iS=Ki1,…,iS∏s(us)isP_{i_1,\ldots,i_S}=K_{i_1,\ldots,i_S}\prod_s (u_s)_{i_s}Pi1​,…,iS​​=Ki1​,…,iS​​∏s​(us​)is​​.

Set r=max⁡s∥(projs)♯P−as∥1r=\max_s\norm{(\mathrm{proj}_s)_\sharp P-\a_s}_1r=maxs​∥(projs​)♯​P−as​∥1​.

Return PPP.

Metric Learning and Inverse OT¶

This section points to inverse problems where the ground cost itself is learned. Such problems are typically bilevel and non-convex, but OT provides useful gradients with respect to the cost.

Metric Learning and Derivatives of OT¶

OT is convex with respect to the measure and concave with respect to the cost. Ground-metric learning was explicitly studied in Cuturi & Avis, 2014, and it connects to the broader metric-learning literature Kulis, 2012Bellet et al., 2015.

Proposition: Derivative with Respect to the Cost

In the discrete setting, assume that the optimal coupling for LC(a,b)\mathcal L_C(a,b)LC​(a,b) is unique and denote it by P⋆(C)P^\star(C)P⋆(C). Then C↦LC(a,b)C\mapsto\mathcal L_C(a,b)C↦LC​(a,b) is differentiable at CCC and

∇CLC(a,b)=P⋆(C).\nabla_C\mathcal L_C(a,b)=P^\star(C).∇C​LC​(a,b)=P⋆(C).
(28)
Proof

The value is the minimum of affine functions of CCC,

LC(a,b)=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨C,P⟩.\mathcal L_C(a,b) = \min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \dotp{C}{P}.LC​(a,b)=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨C,P⟩.
(29)

The envelope theorem, or equivalently Danskin’s theorem, states that the subdifferential with respect to CCC is the convex hull of the optimal couplings. If the optimizer is unique, this subdifferential is the singleton {P⋆(C)}\{P^\star(C)\}{P⋆(C)}, hence the value is differentiable with the displayed gradient.

Thus, if the cost is parameterized as CθC_\thetaCθ​, gradients of losses involving OT values are obtained by backpropagating through the inner product ⟨P⋆(Cθ),∂θCθ⟩\dotp{P^\star(C_\theta)}{\partial_\theta C_\theta}⟨P⋆(Cθ​),∂θ​Cθ​⟩. The difficulty is not differentiating a solved OT problem, but learning a cost for which the resulting matching has the desired semantic behavior; this is a bilevel and usually non-convex optimization problem.

show_book_figure("metric-learning-cost-deformation")

Changing the ground metric changes the optimal coupling. The same red and blue empirical measures are matched with cA(x,y)=(x−y)⊤A(x−y)c_A(x,y)=(x-y)^\top A(x-y)cA​(x,y)=(x−y)⊤A(x−y) for the Euclidean metric and two increasingly anisotropic Mahalanobis metrics. The small gray ellipse shows the unit ball of the metric: directions in which the ellipse is elongated are cheaper, and this deforms the transport segments selected by the OT plan.

The interactive demo lets the anisotropy and orientation of the Mahalanobis cost move. The transport plan is recomputed exactly for the displayed particles, so the segments show how the learned cost changes the matching.

Interactive panel. Use the metric and deformation controls to see how learning the ground cost changes the apparent transport geometry.

Inverse Optimal Transport¶

Inverse OT asks for a ground cost that explains observed matchings or flows as optimal transport plans. In its most direct form, one observes a plan π^\widehat\piπ with marginals (α,β)(\alpha,\beta)(α,β) and seeks a cost ccc such that π^\widehat\piπ is optimal for

inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫c(x,y) dπ(x,y).\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y).π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(30)

This is ill-posed without structure: adding potentials u(x)+v(y)u(x)+v(y)u(x)+v(y) to a cost does not change the set of optimal couplings, and many costs can rationalize the same sparse plan.

A useful statistical methodology is to measure the suboptimality of the observed plan through a Fenchel--Young loss. Write the score as s=−cs=-cs=−c and define the convex regularized prediction value

Gϵ(s)=sup⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫s dπ−ϵKL⁡(π∣α⊗β).G_\epsilon(s) = \sup_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int s\d\pi - \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(\pi\mid\alpha\otimes\beta).Gϵ​(s)=π∈Π(α,β)sup​∫sdπ−ϵKL(π∣α⊗β).
(31)

The Fenchel--Young loss

Lϵ(c;π^)=Gϵ(−c)+Gϵ∗(π^)+∫c dπ^\mathcal L_\epsilon(c;\widehat\pi) = G_\epsilon(-c) + G_\epsilon^*(\widehat\pi) + \int c\d\widehat\piLϵ​(c;π)=Gϵ​(−c)+Gϵ∗​(π)+∫cdπ
(32)

is nonnegative by Fenchel’s inequality and vanishes exactly when π^∈∂Gϵ(−c)\widehat\pi\in\partial G_\epsilon(-c)π∈∂Gϵ​(−c), i.e. when π^\widehat\piπ satisfies the regularized optimality conditions for ccc. Entropic regularization is important here because it makes the forward map smoother and provides gradients with respect to ccc, at the price of a statistical bias Andrade et al., 2025Peyré et al., 2026.

In practice, one restricts the cost to a finite-dimensional model class, often affine:

Cθ=∑r=1RθrC(r),θ∈Θ,C_\theta=\sum_{r=1}^R\theta_r C^{(r)}, \qquad \theta\in\Theta,Cθ​=r=1∑R​θr​C(r),θ∈Θ,
(33)

where Θ\ThetaΘ is convex and the matrices C(r)C^{(r)}C(r) encode features, graph distances or a Mahalanobis parameterization. This viewpoint appears in low-rank and sparse inverse OT models Dupuy et al., 2019Andrade et al., 2024 and in convex formulations for learning OT costs from observed plans Ma et al., 2020Peyré et al., 2026.

Proposition: Convex Dual-Gap Formulation of Inverse OT

Let P^∈U(a,b)\widehat P\in\mathbf U(a,b)P∈U(a,b) be an observed coupling and let CθC_\thetaCθ​ depend affinely on θ∈Θ\theta\in\Thetaθ∈Θ, where Θ\ThetaΘ is convex. The condition that P^\widehat PP is optimal for the cost CθC_\thetaCθ​ is equivalent to the existence of dual potentials (f,g)(f,g)(f,g) such that

fi+gj≤(Cθ)i,jand∑i,jP^i,j((Cθ)i,j−fi−gj)=0.f_i+g_j\leq (C_\theta)_{i,j} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \sum_{i,j}\widehat P_{i,j} \big((C_\theta)_{i,j}-f_i-g_j\big)=0.fi​+gj​≤(Cθ​)i,j​andi,j∑​Pi,j​((Cθ​)i,j​−fi​−gj​)=0.
(34)

Consequently, for a convex regularizer RRR, the noisy inverse problem can be relaxed as the convex program

min⁡θ∈Θ,f,gR(θ)+λ∑i,jP^i,j((Cθ)i,j−fi−gj)\min_{\theta\in\Theta,f,g} R(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i,j}\widehat P_{i,j} \big((C_\theta)_{i,j}-f_i-g_j\big)θ∈Θ,f,gmin​R(θ)+λi,j∑​Pi,j​((Cθ​)i,j​−fi​−gj​)
(35)

subject to fi+gj≤(Cθ)i,jf_i+g_j\leq(C_\theta)_{i,j}fi​+gj​≤(Cθ​)i,j​ for all i,ji,ji,j.

Proof

For a fixed cost CθC_\thetaCθ​, Kantorovich duality gives

min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨Cθ,P⟩=max⁡fi+gj≤(Cθ)i,j⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩.\min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \dotp{C_\theta}{P} = \max_{f_i+g_j\leq(C_\theta)_{i,j}} \dotp{f}{a}+\dotp{g}{b}.P∈U(a,b)min​⟨Cθ​,P⟩=fi​+gj​≤(Cθ​)i,j​max​⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩.
(36)

Since P^\widehat PP has marginals (a,b)(a,b)(a,b), every dual feasible pair satisfies

⟨Cθ,P^⟩−⟨f,a⟩−⟨g,b⟩=∑i,jP^i,j((Cθ)i,j−fi−gj)≥0.\dotp{C_\theta}{\widehat P} - \dotp{f}{a} - \dotp{g}{b} = \sum_{i,j}\widehat P_{i,j} \big((C_\theta)_{i,j}-f_i-g_j\big) \geq0.⟨Cθ​,P⟩−⟨f,a⟩−⟨g,b⟩=i,j∑​Pi,j​((Cθ​)i,j​−fi​−gj​)≥0.
(37)

This nonnegative quantity is exactly the primal-dual gap of P^\widehat PP. It vanishes if and only if P^\widehat PP reaches the dual value and is therefore optimal. If CθC_\thetaCθ​ is affine and Θ\ThetaΘ and RRR are convex, the constraints and objective in (35) are convex.

The formulation is useful because it avoids differentiating through a forward OT solver: it learns a cost by making the observed plan satisfy complementary slackness. In statistical settings, P^\widehat PP is only partially observed or noisy, so one adds sparsity, low-rank, smoothness or metric constraints to select a meaningful cost. For entropic OT, the optimality condition becomes smoother:

P^i,j≈aibjexp⁡ ⁣(fi+gj−(Cθ)i,jϵ),\widehat P_{i,j} \approx a_i b_j \exp\!\left( \frac{f_i+g_j-(C_\theta)_{i,j}}{\epsilon} \right),Pi,j​≈ai​bj​exp(ϵfi​+gj​−(Cθ​)i,j​​),
(38)

which leads to likelihood-based or KL-based convex objectives when CθC_\thetaCθ​ is affine, and connects inverse OT with generalized Sinkhorn iterations and transport-regularized inverse problems Karlsson & Ringh, 2016Ma et al., 2020.

Algorithm: Inverse OT by dual-gap fitting

Input: Observed plan P^∈U(a,b)\widehat P\in\CouplingsD(\a,\b)P∈U(a,b), features C(r)C^{(r)}C(r), feasible set Θ\ThetaΘ, regularizer RRR.

Output: Identified cost Cθ⋆C_{\theta^\star}Cθ⋆​ and potentials (f⋆,g⋆)(f^\star,g^\star)(f⋆,g⋆).

Set parametric cost: Cθ=∑rθrC(r).C_\theta=\sum_r\theta_r C^{(r)}.Cθ​=∑r​θr​C(r).

Let (θ⋆,f⋆,g⋆)(\theta^\star,f^\star,g^\star)(θ⋆,f⋆,g⋆) be a minimizer of min⁡θ∈Θ,f,gR(θ)+λ∑i,jP^i,j((Cθ)i,j−fi−gj)\min_{\theta\in\Theta,f,g} R(\theta)+\lambda \sum_{i,j}\widehat P_{i,j}\big((C_\theta)_{i,j}-f_i-g_j\big)minθ∈Θ,f,g​R(θ)+λ∑i,j​Pi,j​((Cθ​)i,j​−fi​−gj​)

Subject to fi+gj≤(Cθ)i,jfor all (i,j).f_i+g_j\leq(C_\theta)_{i,j} \qquad\text{for all }(i,j).fi​+gj​≤(Cθ​)i,j​for all (i,j). Return θ⋆\theta^\starθ⋆, Cθ⋆C_{\theta^\star}Cθ⋆​, and (f⋆,g⋆)(f^\star,g^\star)(f⋆,g⋆).

Weak Optimal Transport¶

Weak OT relaxes the cost so that it depends on the conditional distribution of destinations rather than only on pointwise pairs. It is useful when a source point is allowed to choose a randomized response and the model only penalizes an aggregate of that response, such as its conditional mean.

Barycentric Projection of a Coupling¶

The first object to isolate is the map obtained by collapsing each conditional law to its barycenter.

Definition: Barycentric Projection of a Coupling

Let α,β∈P2(Rd)\alpha,\beta\in\mathcal P_2(\RR^d)α,β∈P2​(Rd) and let π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β). Disintegrate π\piπ with respect to its first marginal as π( dx, dy)=πx( dy)α( dx)\pi(\d x,\d y)=\pi_x(\d y)\alpha(\d x)π(dx,dy)=πx​(dy)α(dx). The barycentric projection of π\piπ is the map

Tˉπ(x):=∫Rdy dπx(y),βˉπ:=(Tˉπ)♯α.\bar T_\pi(x) \eqdef \int_{\RR^d}y\d\pi_x(y), \qquad \bar\beta_\pi \eqdef (\bar T_\pi)_\sharp\alpha.Tˉπ​(x):=∫Rd​ydπx​(y),βˉ​π​:=(Tˉπ​)♯​α.
(39)

The projected target βˉπ\bar\beta_\piβˉ​π​ records the distribution of conditional means, not the full second marginal. Thus it is generally different from β\betaβ; if π=(Id,T)♯α\pi=(\Id,T)_\sharp\alphaπ=(Id,T)♯​α is induced by a map, then Tˉπ=T\bar T_\pi=TTˉπ​=T and βˉπ=β\bar\beta_\pi=\betaβˉ​π​=β. This projection is not an optimal map for an arbitrary coupling: a deterministic rotation of a radially symmetric source, for example, projects to the rotation itself, whereas the optimal map from the source to itself is the identity. The useful positive statement is attached to quadratic optimal plans, as in the tangent-space viewpoint on W2\Wass_2W2​ developed by Ambrosio, Gigli and Savare Ambrosio et al., 2006.

Proposition: Barycentric Projection of a Quadratic Optimal Plan

Let π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β) be optimal for the quadratic cost ∥x−y∥2\norm{x-y}^2∥x−y∥2 between α,β∈P2(Rd)\alpha,\beta\in\mathcal P_2(\RR^d)α,β∈P2​(Rd), and define Tˉπ\bar T_\piTˉπ​ and βˉπ\bar\beta_\piβˉ​π​ by (39). Then (Id,Tˉπ)♯α(\Id,\bar T_\pi)_\sharp\alpha(Id,Tˉπ​)♯​α is an optimal coupling between α\alphaα and βˉπ\bar\beta_\piβˉ​π​. Equivalently, Tˉπ\bar T_\piTˉπ​ is a quadratic optimal transport map from α\alphaα to the projected target βˉπ\bar\beta_\piβˉ​π​.

Proof

By the cyclic-monotonicity characterization of quadratic optimality, π\piπ is concentrated on a ccc-cyclically monotone set Γ\GammaΓ for c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2. This means that every finite cycle (xi,yi)i=1m⊂Γ(x_i,y_i)_{i=1}^m\subset\Gamma(xi​,yi​)i=1m​⊂Γ satisfies

∑i=1m⟨xi,yi⟩≥∑i=1m⟨xi,yi+1⟩,ym+1=y1.\sum_{i=1}^m\dotp{x_i}{y_i} \geq \sum_{i=1}^m\dotp{x_i}{y_{i+1}}, \qquad y_{m+1}=y_1.i=1∑m​⟨xi​,yi​⟩≥i=1∑m​⟨xi​,yi+1​⟩,ym+1​=y1​.
(40)

After changing the disintegration on an α\alphaα-negligible set, πx\pi_xπx​ is supported on the section Γx={y:(x,y)∈Γ}\Gamma_x=\{y:(x,y)\in\Gamma\}Γx​={y:(x,y)∈Γ} for α\alphaα-almost every xxx. Choose x1,…,xmx_1,\ldots,x_mx1​,…,xm​ in this full-measure set and independently sample Yi∼πxiY_i\sim\pi_{x_i}Yi​∼πxi​​. Applying the cyclic inequality to (xi,Yi)(x_i,Y_i)(xi​,Yi​) and taking expectations gives

∑i=1m⟨xi,Tˉπ(xi)⟩≥∑i=1m⟨xi,Tˉπ(xi+1)⟩.\sum_{i=1}^m\dotp{x_i}{\bar T_\pi(x_i)} \geq \sum_{i=1}^m\dotp{x_i}{\bar T_\pi(x_{i+1})}.i=1∑m​⟨xi​,Tˉπ​(xi​)⟩≥i=1∑m​⟨xi​,Tˉπ​(xi+1​)⟩.
(41)

Thus (Id,Tˉπ)♯α(\Id,\bar T_\pi)_\sharp\alpha(Id,Tˉπ​)♯​α is concentrated on a cyclically monotone graph. By the cyclic-monotonicity characterization of quadratic optimality, this plan is optimal between its two marginals.

Weak transport costs use the same disintegration but allow the objective to depend on the whole conditional law, or on summaries such as the barycentric projection (39). The framework was introduced through general transport costs and weak transport inequalities, with existence, duality and optimality conditions developed on Polish spaces Gozlan et al., 2017Backhoff Veraguas et al., 2018. For a weak cost C:X×P(Y)→R∪{+∞}C:\X\times\mathcal P(\Y)\to\RR\cup\{+\infty\}C:X×P(Y)→R∪{+∞}, the weak OT value is

WC(α,β):=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫C(x,πx) dα(x).\mathcal W_C(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int C(x,\pi_x)\d\alpha(x).WC​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫C(x,πx​)dα(x).
(42)

The classical Kantorovich problem is recovered when C(x,ν)=∫c(x,y) dν(y)C(x,\nu)=\int c(x,y)\d\nu(y)C(x,ν)=∫c(x,y)dν(y), because the objective then becomes ∫c(x,y) dπ(x,y)\int c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y)∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y). The genuinely weak behavior starts when CCC is nonlinear in ν\nuν.

Proposition: Weak Kantorovich Duality

Assume that X,Y\X,\YX,Y are compact metric spaces and that C(x,ν)C(x,\nu)C(x,ν) is lower semicontinuous, bounded from below and convex in ν\nuν, with the standard qualification assumptions ensuring Fenchel--Rockafellar duality. For g∈C(Y)g\in\mathcal C(\Y)g∈C(Y) define the weak CCC-transform

gC(x):=inf⁡ν∈P(Y){C(x,ν)−∫g(y) dν(y)}.g^C(x) \eqdef \inf_{\nu\in\mathcal P(\Y)} \left\{ C(x,\nu)-\int g(y)\d\nu(y) \right\}.gC(x):=ν∈P(Y)inf​{C(x,ν)−∫g(y)dν(y)}.
(43)

Then

WC(α,β)=sup⁡g∈C(Y){∫gC(x) dα(x)+∫g(y) dβ(y)}.\mathcal W_C(\alpha,\beta) = \sup_{g\in\mathcal C(\Y)} \left\{ \int g^C(x)\d\alpha(x) + \int g(y)\d\beta(y) \right\}.WC​(α,β)=g∈C(Y)sup​{∫gC(x)dα(x)+∫g(y)dβ(y)}.
(44)

When C(x,ν)=∫c(x,y) dν(y)C(x,\nu)=\int c(x,y)\d\nu(y)C(x,ν)=∫c(x,y)dν(y), this reduces to the usual Kantorovich dual with gC(x)=inf⁡y(c(x,y)−g(y))g^C(x)=\inf_y(c(x,y)-g(y))gC(x)=infy​(c(x,y)−g(y)).

Proof

For any coupling π\piπ and any g∈C(Y)g\in\mathcal C(\Y)g∈C(Y), the definition of gCg^CgC gives

C(x,πx)≥gC(x)+∫g(y) dπx(y).C(x,\pi_x) \geq g^C(x) + \int g(y)\d\pi_x(y).C(x,πx​)≥gC(x)+∫g(y)dπx​(y).
(45)

After integration with respect to α\alphaα, the second term becomes ∫g dβ\int g\d\beta∫gdβ because the second marginal of π\piπ is β\betaβ. This proves weak duality.

For the reverse inequality, consider the convex minimization over probability kernels x↦πxx\mapsto\pi_xx↦πx​ with the affine constraint ∫πx dα(x)=β\int \pi_x\d\alpha(x)=\beta∫πx​dα(x)=β. Fenchel--Rockafellar duality gives a continuous Lagrange multiplier ggg for this marginal constraint. Minimizing the Lagrangian over each conditional law gives exactly the pointwise term gC(x)g^C(x)gC(x), while the multiplier contributes ∫g dβ\int g\d\beta∫gdβ. The compactness, lower semicontinuity, convexity and qualification assumptions ensure no duality gap.

Proposition: Barycentric Weak Transport Is Weaker than W2\Wass_2W2​

Let α,β∈P2(Rd)\alpha,\beta\in\mathcal P_2(\RR^d)α,β∈P2​(Rd) and define

Cbar(x,ν)=∥x−∫y dν(y)∥2.C_{\mathrm{bar}}(x,\nu) = \norm{x-\int y\d\nu(y)}^2.Cbar​(x,ν)=∥∥​x−∫ydν(y)∥∥​2.
(46)

Equivalently, for a coupling π\piπ, the integrand is ∥x−Tˉπ(x)∥2\norm{x-\bar T_\pi(x)}^2∥∥​x−Tˉπ​(x)∥∥​2. Then

WCbar(α,β)≤W22(α,β).\mathcal W_{C_{\mathrm{bar}}}(\alpha,\beta) \leq \Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta).WCbar​​(α,β)≤W22​(α,β).
(47)
Proof

Let π\piπ be any coupling and disintegrate it as πxα\pi_x\alphaπx​α. By Jensen’s inequality,

∥x−Tˉπ(x)∥2≤∫∥x−y∥2 dπx(y).\norm{x-\bar T_\pi(x)}^2 \leq \int\norm{x-y}^2\d\pi_x(y).∥∥​x−Tˉπ​(x)∥∥​2≤∫∥x−y∥2dπx​(y).
(48)

Integrating in xxx gives ∫Cbar(x,πx) dα(x)≤∫∥x−y∥2 dπ(x,y)\int C_{\mathrm{bar}}(x,\pi_x)\d\alpha(x)\leq \int\norm{x-y}^2\d\pi(x,y)∫Cbar​(x,πx​)dα(x)≤∫∥x−y∥2dπ(x,y). Taking the infimum over π\piπ proves the claim.

show_book_figure("weak-ot-barycentric-projection")

Weak barycentric transport on a small disk-to-annulus coupling. The left panel shows the full conditional laws: each red source atom splits its mass among several blue target atoms, with segment thickness proportional to transported mass. The right panel collapses each conditional law πx\pi_xπx​ to its barycenter Tˉπ(x)=∫y dπx(y)\bar T_\pi(x)=\int y\d\pi_x(y)Tˉπ​(x)=∫ydπx​(y), shown in violet. The barycentric weak cost only sees the red-to-violet displacement, and therefore ignores the conditional spread around each barycenter.

The interactive demo lets each source point split toward several targets. Increasing the split count or spread usually increases the full quadratic cost while the weak barycentric cost can remain much smaller.

Interactive panel. Use the spread and barycentric controls to compare full weak conditional laws with their barycentric projections.

The barycentric cost is the canonical example to keep in mind: admissibility still constrains the full conditional laws to have second marginal β\betaβ, but the objective only charges the displacement from xxx to Tˉπ(x)\bar T_\pi(x)Tˉπ​(x) and ignores the conditional variance around this barycenter. This connects weak OT with martingale transport, Strassen-type convex-order constraints, barycentric projections and learning problems where conditional averages are meaningful objects.

References¶
  1. Agueh, M., & Carlier, G. (2011). Barycenters in the Wasserstein space. SIAM Journal on Mathematical Analysis, 43(2), 904–924.
  2. Carlier, G., & Ekeland, I. (2010). Matching for teams. Economic Theory, 42(2), 397–418.
  3. Anderes, E., Borgwardt, S., & Miller, J. (2016). Discrete Wasserstein barycenters: optimal transport for discrete data. Mathematical Methods of Operations Research, 84(2), 389–409.
  4. Álvarez Esteban, P. C., del Barrio, E., Cuesta-Albertos, J., & Matrán, C. (2016). A fixed-point approach to barycenters in Wasserstein space. Journal of Mathematical Analysis and Applications, 441(2), 744–762.
  5. Le Gouic, T., & Loubes, J.-M. (2016). Existence and consistency of Wasserstein barycenters. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 168, 901–917.
  6. Bhatia, R., Jain, T., & Lim, Y. (2019). On the Bures–Wasserstein distance between positive definite matrices. Expositiones Mathematicae, 37(2), 165–191. 10.1016/j.exmath.2018.01.002
  7. Cuturi, M., & Doucet, A. (2014). Fast computation of Wasserstein barycenters. Proceedings of the 31st International Conference on Machine Learning, 32, 685–693. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v32/cuturi14.html
  8. Cuturi, M., & Peyré, G. (2016). A smoothed dual approach for variational Wasserstein problems. SIAM Journal on Imaging Sciences, 9(1), 320–343.
  9. Benamou, J.-D., Carlier, G., Cuturi, M., Nenna, L., & Peyré, G. (2015). Iterative Bregman projections for regularized transportation problems. SIAM Journal on Scientific Computing, 37(2), A1111–A1138.
  10. Solomon, J., De Goes, F., Peyré, G., Cuturi, M., Butscher, A., Nguyen, A., Du, T., & Guibas, L. (2015). Convolutional Wasserstein distances: efficient optimal transportation on geometric domains. ACM Transactions on Graphics, 34(4), 66:1-66:11.
  11. Cuturi, M., & Avis, D. (2014). Ground metric learning. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 15, 533–564.
  12. Kulis, B. (2012). Metric learning: a survey. Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, 5(4), 287–364.
  13. Bellet, A., Habrard, A., & Sebban, M. (2015). Metric learning. Synthesis Lectures on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning, 9(1), 1–151.
  14. Andrade, F., Peyré, G., & Poon, C. (2025). Learning from Samples: Inverse Problems over Measures via Sharpened Fenchel–Young Losses. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2505.07124.
  15. Peyré, G., Poon, C., & Tron, O. (2026). Curvature of optimal transport with respect to the cost and applications to inverse optimal transport. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2604.22670.
